headline: CRUDE CRASHES AS CATTLE HOLD GAINS
subheadline: Oil's 4.4% plunge reverses Tuesday's energy rally while livestock extends breakout.
lead: Crude oil crashed $4.81 to $104.22 — its worst single-day drop in three months — but cattle shrugged off the energy selloff and held yesterday's breakout above $255. The divergence says something important: cattle's momentum has fund support that crude's flash rally never earned. Live futures closed at $255.10, down just 70 cents from yesterday's 52-week high.
teaser: Crude crashes 4.4% but cattle hold the breakout — funds picking their spots.

one_number.context: WTI crude's $4.81 crash erased Tuesday's entire rally and then some. The move started overnight in Asia and accelerated through New York trading — classic momentum reversal after Tuesday's parabolic spike failed to hold. Energy complex is learning the difference between a squeeze and a trend.

yesterdays_call.summary: Hold remaining fed cattle inventory as momentum accelerates with fund support.
yesterdays_call.note: Cattle held the breakout even as crude crashed — exactly the kind of independent strength that confirms fund conviction.

sections.title: Cattle Hold Breakout
sections.body: Live cattle closed at $255.10, down just 0.7% despite crude oil's 4.4% crash — the kind of independent strength that separates real breakouts from false ones. Yesterday's 52-week high at $255.80 is holding as support, not resistance. Managed money added long positions for a third straight session while feedlot margins expanded another $15 per head. The funds that missed Tuesday's energy spike are rotating into livestock momentum that actually has fundamentals behind it.
sections.bottom_line: Breakout confirmed — cattle acting independently of energy selloff shows genuine strength.
sections.farmer_action: Price remaining summer cattle inventory above $250 — the breakout has legs.

sections.title: Energy Complex Reverses
sections.body: WTI crude crashed $4.81 to $104.22 — the sharpest single-day drop since January and a complete reversal of Tuesday's parabolic rally. The selloff started in Asia when inventory data showed builds instead of draws, then accelerated when funds who bought Tuesday's spike hit stops. Natural gas followed crude down, falling 1.0% to $2.61. The energy complex is learning that momentum without fundamentals doesn't last — crude's still up 12% month-to-date, but yesterday's $109 high looks like a blow-off top.
sections.bottom_line: Tuesday's energy spike was a squeeze, not a trend — fundamentals matter.

sections.title: Grains Track Lower
sections.body: Corn fell 3¾ cents to $4.75 as crude's crash pulled input costs lower and funds liquidated commodity positions across the board. December corn dropped 3½ cents to $4.96, keeping the July-December carry at 21 cents — still wide enough to reward storage. Soybeans slid 11¼ cents to $11.92 while soybean meal dropped $3.20 to $320 as crush margins compressed. The grain complex is acting like a commodity basket trade again, not individual markets with separate fundamentals.
sections.bottom_line: Grains following energy down — basket trade trumping individual fundamentals today.

sections.title: Metals Rally Solo
sections.body: Gold jumped $78 to $4,647 and silver spiked $1.59 to $74.09 as the dollar's strength wavered and energy's crash raised recession fears. The metals are acting as the safe-haven trade while commodities broadly sold off — classic flight-to-quality behavior when energy leads the complex lower. Gold's 1.7% gain stands out in a sea of red across agricultural and energy futures. The precious metals complex is pricing different risks than the rest of the commodity space.
sections.bottom_line: Metals rally while commodities crash — classic safe-haven divergence.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / WTI crude ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: Cattle holding $255 while crude crashed to $104 pushed this ratio to its widest since February. When livestock decouples from energy like this, it signals the breakout has fundamental support beyond the commodity momentum trade.

basis.headline: Corn basis softens as energy costs retreat
basis.body: Eastern Corn Belt basis eased 2-3 cents as crude's crash pulled ethanol margins lower and reduced demand urgency. Western basis held firmer on continued rail congestion, but the energy selloff is removing the cost-push premium that's supported corn basis all month. Local elevator pricing reflecting the input cost relief.

weekly_thread.question: Can cattle extend the breakout above $255 without energy sector support?
weekly_thread.status_text: Today answered it definitively — cattle held the breakout while crude crashed 4.4%, proving the livestock rally has independent fundamental support that doesn't need energy momentum.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Commodity Divergence Matters More Than Correlation
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 4.4% crude crash while cattle held $255 breakout illustrates a key market principle: correlation breaks down at inflection points. When cattle was trading $240-245 range in March, it moved in lockstep with energy — both responding to broad commodity flows. But once cattle broke $250 resistance with fund support, it developed its own momentum based on feedlot margins and beef demand. The divergence today confirms cattle's rally has legs independent of energy sector performance. Watch for similar decoupling in grains once planting pressure builds.

watch_list.desc: Cattle close: above $253 keeps breakout intact, below $250 questions momentum
USDA Crop Progress: corn planting pace versus 5-year average determines weather premium
EIA crude inventory: build above 2M barrels confirms demand weakness behind today's crash

daily_quote.text: Take good care of the earth. It was not given to you by your parents. It was loaned to you by your children.

source_summary: CBOT/CME futures, overnight Asian sessions, EIA inventory estimates
